Fundamental similarities and differences between ETH and ETHA

The Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust and the iShares Ethereum Trust are two funds that, to my mind, represent the most attractive spot holders of the Ethereum cryptocurrency. That is their main similarity, and it is why their total return performance correlates with each other. In spite of this significant similarity, ETH and ETHA have a number of fundamental differences. These differences can be used to determine which of these ETFs is more suitable for a particular type of investor. There are three key points that distinguish ETH from ETHA.

Point #1: Commission

This is the simplest criterion for determining that ETH is the cheaper option, as its commission is 0.15% per annum, whereas ETHA's commission is 0.25%. However, when the latter fund was launched, there was a preferential period with a commission of 0.12%. This concession lasted until July 2025, after which it reverted to the full 0.25%.

Point #2: Fund size

Based on liquidity indicators, ETHA is the undisputed leader among the analyzed Ethereum ETFs. Its trading volume is huge (average daily dollar volume of $1.21 billion compared to $192.88 million for ETH), and its spread is minimal. When actively trading or investing large sums, this is an important criterion. Nevertheless, ETH cannot be considered an illiquid fund. It was created as a spin-off from the main Grayscale fund to offer the market a cheaper alternative for investing in Ethereum. While ETHA has $11.43 billion in AUM, ETH has $2.24 billion. That makes it the largest Ethereum-based ETF on the market.

Point #3: Staking

Perhaps the most significant difference between ETH and ETHA is the presence of a staking feature. In October 2025, it was reported that the Grayscale management team had begun the staking process in order to generate passive income from the fund's spot Ethereum holdings. Now, the fund will passively place assets through institutional custodians and a network of validator providers to ensure the security of the underlying protocols while maintaining the long-term stability of the network. Staking refers to the process of locking cryptocurrency in a blockchain network to verify transactions and earn rewards. BlackRock (iShares) does not use this feature, as the fund maintains a conservative position on owning real Ethereum.

Why do I choose Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust?

If maximum reliability and choosing the best financial brand are your most important criteria, I believe ETHA is the right choice. BlackRock is the largest asset manager in the world. Furthermore, ETHA is a good choice if you are investing a very large amount of money and actively trading this asset on the market. In this case, the liquidity of trades and minimal spreads are important to you.

I prefer ETH, mainly because of the potential for additional returns from Ethereum staking. It is worth considering the advantage of lower fees (0.15% vs. 0.25%), a difference of 0.1%. But, given the movements in the price of Ethereum, the difference is not that significant. Since I do not intend to actively trade cryptocurrency ETFs, spreads and trading liquidity are not that important to me.

Speaking about the potential of Ethereum staking, let me elaborate on this in more detail. Today, this mechanism is offered by various crypto exchanges, operators, and crypto wallets. The Ethereum staking feature is available on Ledger's hardware wallet. Today, investing in Ethereum staking through its account can yield up to 3.61% per annum. To do this, there are eight intermediaries, each with its own terms and conditions for staking.

Staking is also offered by major exchanges. Coinbase, for example, has more modest terms for financing under its ETH staking program, providing an opportunity to earn an annual return on investment of 1.9%. The crypto exchange says that on December 4, 2025, there were a total of 35.7 million tokens staked. However, this data is for all operators. No public data on Coinbase's share is available. According to various estimates, it's about 2–4 million tokens. Other terms are offered by the world's largest crypto exchange, Binance. Its Ethereum staking program offers the opportunity to earn a return of 2.6% per annum. But the downside of this product is that income payments are made in WBETH tokens. The crypto exchange says that as of December 4, 2025, a combined total of 3.166 million tokens have been invested through their infrastructure.

Risks of the investment thesis

Like other Ethereum ETFs, buying ETH comes with risks related to the volatility of the cryptocurrency's price. The fundamental analysis I did points to some big factors that could push up the asset's price. But from a technical analysis perspective, I do not rule out an alternative forecast, which suggests that Ethereum prices could fall to the support zone ($2,112-$2,463). Considering that the current price of Ethereum is $3,200, this could represent a potential decline of 23% to 34%. Given the 100% correlation between ETH and the price of Ethereum, the decline in the value of the ETF would be the same, at 23% to 34%. That is why choosing the right moment and entry point is important for every investor. I recommend using the Dollar Cost Averaging (DCA) strategy. With this strategy, you don't try to guess the “local bottom” of the market; you simply divide your planned capital into equal parts (for example, into 2-4 positions) and enter each trade within 2-4 months, independent of the price of Ethereum.

Conclusion

Therefore, based on my fundamental analysis of a direct comparison between ETH and ETHA, it is possible to identify significant differences between these seemingly similar spot ETFs with Ethereum as their underlying asset. Between these two funds, I chose ETH because this asset has the potential to generate additional returns from staking cryptocurrency holdings. I estimate that this operation could bring an additional 2%–4% per annum, which is a significant difference compared to other spot ETFs on Ethereum. Given that the total returns of each fund in this market are similar (the difference is no more than 0.1% per annum), Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF has a competitive advantage in the long term.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Why To Watch Bond Yields... In Japan
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It’s time to pay close attention to government bond yields - in Japan.

Check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the yield on the 10-year Japanese government bond. As you can see, it has been soaring lately. It recently topped 1.9%, its highest since July 2007. The equivalent yield here in the US is 4.09%.

Japanese 10-Year Vs. US 10-Year (Yield in %)

(Data by YCharts)

Rising inflation in a country known for decades of deflation is one driving factor. Another catalyst is a series of comments from Bank of Japan governor Kazuo Ueda implying a rate hike could be coming. That runs counter to expectations for a rate cut at next week’s Federal Reserve meeting in Washington.

So, let’s get to the million-dollar question: Why should you care?

First, because global bond markets are interconnected. The recent jump in Japanese yields put upward pressure on yields in the US, too - not to mention on yields elsewhere around the world.

Second, because Japan is the largest foreign holder of US government debt. Japanese investors have long poured money into US bonds (and stocks) to capture the higher yields and returns offered here versus at home. But if the yield divergence between Japanese and US bonds keeps narrowing, it could lead to capital flows out of US markets.

That would boost the Japanese yen versus the US dollar, while also increasing borrowing costs for US consumers and businesses. It could also put downward pressure on red-hot growth sectors like tech and AI.

So, yes, this is something to keep an eye on. I’ve long had a “Three ‘F’ Rule” when it comes to bond yields and stock prices. When yields rise far enough, fast enough, and for long enough, it can become a real problem for equities. That's true at home and abroad!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: The 4 Dangerous Optical Illusions That Keep Most Dividend Investors Poor
data.description:
Optical illusions fool people because they make them think that they see one thing when the reality is actually quite different. This applies to income investing as well, as investors are often misled by what they see on the surface of income-generating securities.

In today's article, I am going to share some of the biggest optical illusions that fool income investors into making poor capital allocation decisions.

The Biggest Illusion: Track Record Tunnel Vision

The first one I am going to discuss is putting way too much emphasis on an income security's track record. Sure, income-generating securities that have total return charts that feature general movement up and to the right look very attractive, and sometimes a track record is an important indicator. At a bare minimum, it is not something to ignore, for sure.

However, it is not something that should have enormous emphasis placed on it when selecting a stock, whether it be the stock price chart or the dividend track record. For example, many investors thought that the late NextEra Energy Partners (XIFR), AT&T Inc. (T), V.F. Corporation (VFC), 3M Company (MMM), or Walgreens Boots Alliance were all great income investment opportunities because they had impressive dividend growth track records. However, all of those ended up slashing their dividends and shareholders paid the price.

Additionally, today, many investors are piling into funds like JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), or the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) and others like them. This is being driven in part by the fact that they have delivered very strong total return performance compared to popular dividend ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) in recent years.

While that is all well and good. The fact of the matter is that these funds have experienced stronger performance because their underlying indexes, which are more heavily weighted towards mega-cap tech and AI stocks, have outperformed in recent years since they have launched, whereas value and dividend stocks, Global X SuperDividend™ U.S. ETF (DIV), have been out of favor, which is what SCHD and other dividend ETFs tend to focus on. However, if there were to be a market rotation into value and DIV stocks, the performance would be inverted, and these covered call ETFs would likely significantly underperform the likes of SCHD. In fact, the covered call funds have dramatically underperformed their underlying indexes, SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY), Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ):

Additionally, some investors have steered clear of MLPs, Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), like Energy Transfer LP (ET) and Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (PAA), Plains GP Holdings, L.P. (PAGP) after they slashed their distributions in the wake of COVID-19. However, by obsessing over their spotty distribution track records, investors have missed out on significant total returns since then by failing to look at the forward-looking prospects of the business.

Therefore, simply looking at a stock chart or even a dividend growth chart can be very misleading when analyzing a company, and much more investigation is generally needed to be able to determine if a company or even a fund is worth investing in at the moment.

The Myth of High Yields in Some CEFs

Another major optical illusion is thinking that just because a CEF pays out a huge distribution, it is truly an income-generating security. While, in general, stocks and ETFs that pay out high dividend yields are income investments, many CEFs, such as the Liberty All-Star Equity (USA) and Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX), are not actual dividend CEFs because their underlying holdings are not major dividend players and they do not even write covered calls on their holdings to generate monthly cash flow. Therefore, their substantial cash payouts to shareholders come from liquidating their underlying holdings over time to lock in capital gains.

While this distinction does not matter too much in terms of long-term total returns, there is one big drawback. Namely, there is a sequence of returns risk. This is because if the market crashes and remains suppressed for an extended period of time, these stocks could be forced to sell a large portion of their underlying securities at steep losses to fund their distributions. Alternatively, they would have to cut or even eliminate their distributions in the meantime. In contrast, if the funds were to pay out their distributions that were fully generated by the dividends from their underlying holdings, they would never have to sell their underlying holdings and could simply return the cash flow to shareholders.

The reason this is significant is similar to how a company that can fully fund their capital expenditures and business operations from internally generated cash flows is on a much more sustainable financial footing than one that has to do so by going to the debt or equity markets to raise additional capital to do so. The first approach does not have sequence of returns risk in that they are never dependent on the market's mood to deliver cash flow to shareholders. In contrast, the other is dependent on the mood of Mr. Market to be able to do so without having to take a very unfavorable market price at certain points in the cycle in order to continue sending cash flow to investors.

Therefore, those who invest in high-yielding CEFs with the assumption that they are durable dividend payers similar to actual cash distributions that are supported by underlying cash flows, such as midstream businesses like Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) or REITs, Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ), like Realty Income Corporation (O) or even some ETFs like the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), they are potentially setting themselves up to be significantly disappointed. If at some point in the future market volatility messes up the sequence of returns profile and forces their underlying funds to slash their distributions or significantly and permanently impair their NAV.

The Deception of Leverage

Another major optical illusion is the notion that leverage enhances long-term total returns and dividend income. Yes, it is true that if a stock always goes up, leverage does enhance returns. It is also true that as long as the underlying holdings in a portfolio generate higher income yields than the interest cost of the leverage, they can also enhance yields. This is one reason why many high-yielding CEFs can support their distributions, as the significant leverage they take on does juice their cash flow yields internally.

However, as Warren Buffett once cautioned, one of the only ways that a smart person can go broke is through leverage. This is because when you take on significant leverage, especially if it is exposed to margin call-type conditions, you are effectively playing Russian roulette with your portfolio. Sure, the market may go up or at least be relatively stable for an extended period of time, and interest rates remain relatively low, and therefore you can enhance your returns, especially your cash flow yields, by applying them to your portfolio or even simply the fund you are invested in.

However, eventually a black swan scenario will emerge in which the underlying holdings of that fund will likely crash, which could push it into a margin call territory, thereby forcing it to liquidate some of its positions at rock-bottom prices. This, in turn, would likely permanently impair the fund's NAV as well as its cash flow-generating ability and likely lead to a dividend cut. Therefore, leveraging up your portfolio either through margin directly or through simply buying heavily leveraged CEFs and even ETFs can look good on the surface for a while, but in the end, it will likely bite you and lead to painful capital and income losses.

Covered Call Distributions: Inferior Quality

The fourth major optical illusion is that covered call distributions are just as high-quality forms of income as a regular dividend payout supported by underlying cash flows from the company or the fund's holdings. This is incorrect because covered calls have a couple of negative impacts. One, or have a couple of major cons relative to real dividends. One, they are less dependable because their call premiums are often dictated by implied volatility, which by its very definition can vary widely over time, thereby leading to significant variances in the distribution. Premiums that they generate for distributions to shareholders from covered call funds, month to month.

Additionally, they cap upside for the fund, thereby leading not only to lower capital appreciation and the risk of long-term cash erosion but also may force the fund to liquidate some of its underlying holdings to satisfy options contracts that expire in the money, which can also lead to long-term dwindling of the underlying shares held by the fund, which makes it little to no better than simply holding a low-cost diversified ETF, Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF Shares (VTI) like the S&P 500 (SP500) or the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and liquidating your shares regularly over time to fund living expenses. Additionally, these funds tend to charge much higher expense ratios than simply holding the underlying fund or even holding a low-cost index fund like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) does.

Finally, these funds have little basis for growing their dividends over time on a consistent basis, whereas dividend growth funds like SCHD and VIG grow their dividends consistently year after year at a rate that beats inflation. Therefore, while their yields may be meaningfully lower than those of these covered call ETFs, their yields tend to be on a much more sustainable and inflation-beating standing.

It also bears mentioning that qualified dividends tend to earn much more favorable tax treatment than distributions from covered call ETFs, as even more tax-advantaged covered call ETFs like QQQI still have a portion of their distributions that are taxed at short-term capital gains and or ordinary income rates.

Investor Takeaway

Between an overemphasis on total return and dividend track records, the false assumption that every high-yielding CEF is truly a dividend investment rather than a return of capital fund, too much complacency about the risks associated with leveraged CEFs and ETFs, and a misunderstanding about the true (inferior) nature of covered call ETF distributions compared to qualified dividends from dividend growth stocks and funds, income investors regularly get fooled by several optical illusions.

While there are times when there is a place for investing in leveraged funds, high-yield CEFs, and/or covered call ETFs as part of a diversified income portfolio, most of the time, investing in dividend growth ETFs and blue chip stocks is the soundest way to sustainably compound passive income and wealth over the long term. This is why they constitute the vast majority of our 7-8% yielding portfolios that have delivered strong long-term total returns and dividend growth for us at High Yield Investor.
